You young ones grew up with the Diet, but back in my day, no one even knew what it was.
We thought the Diet was going to revamp the bureaucracy as soon as they took office, but it took them a few years to get around to it.
Back then, all we'd heard was that the members of the Diet were to be chosen from among the commoners. The entire Southern Court was in an uproar, and then that poor fool, Koretou, emerged.
The candidate?
Yes. He had a whole heap of political views, but his core aim was to drive us, the Kinsekikai, out of Kajimachi.
He roused a bunch of useless rabble and had them shouting and yelling outside Kajimachi all day and night. It made it impossible for us to do business.
The Old Man, no... not just him, the whole Kinsekikai didn't take Koretou seriously at first, but every day, he recruited more and more troublemakers.
So... you decided to get rid of him.
Yes, and you know the rest of the story, of course. After Koretou died, the Kinsekikai learned what real public fury looks like.
That was also when I learned that to the Four Great Clans, the members of the Diet are nothing but stage props...
But to us... they're people who can decide our fate with a single word.
With all due respect, you could just come out and say it: if Chairman Tessai refuses to yield, you'll need Hannya at the succession ceremony.
I say, sometimes you really do sound just like him.
Of course, Tessai lording it over me is one thing. Hearing it from someone as steadfast and frank as you is another thing entirely.
In truth, once the Kinseki Group splits from the Kinsekikai, I obviously can't stay on as chairman. Is there anyone else, besides you, who can take my place?
The day I step into politics and build a power base of my own, you and your Kinsekikai won't be forgotten.
Mifune bursts out laughing to himself.
The woman before him remains expressionless. Is it habitual impassivity, incredulity, or mere indifference to Mifune's grand vision? There's no way to tell.
For now, Momoka is still our top priority.
She knows too much, and she's in cahoots with that information broker.
She even leapt out a window just to keep Sanoguchi's broken blade on her. Don't you understand what that means?
Mifune-san——
You were too kind-hearted to kill her, and you were willing to admit that to me. That's good. I can promise you this much: we can reconsider our plan to infect her with Oripathy.
First, however, you must bring her to me.
That broken blade she's carrying.... we must recover its guard, no matter the cost.
Secondly, there's no way she can continue her career as an entertainer. From now on, she'll have to remain firmly under our control.
Are you okay with that?
You are most magnanimous.
No. When you bring her here, she should be the one thanking you.
The sun slowly climbs overhead.
Although no other visitors are present, the garden no longer seems so empty and quiet, with four humans and a kami walking through it.

Kichisei begins thrusting forward anything and everything she has that a woman could conceivably scrawl a signature on. Startled by the sudden barrage, Momoka keeps her autographing hand moving without pause.
On the other side of the courtyard, the kannushi cradles the kami, who only moments ago was darting about. The kami speaks to Hoshiguma.
You must understand, the kami was the one who wanted to play games with you. As a kannushi, I could only stand and watch—I couldn't interfere. Please forgive me, Hoshiguma-san.
Well, you sure were happy to watch, weren't you?
Was I?
I think so.
Wholeheartedly serving the kami is bound to lift one's mood. I imagine you felt it too just now, Hoshiguma-san—and you, Momoka-san.
I'm more interested in why It broke into my house in middle of the night.
What do you mean? You talk like I'm some kind of dangerous fiend. Wouldn't you think it was strange if someone was cleaning out junk from a cursed house, and then went to sleep in said haunted house?
Just because it's a haunted house? There are so many killings in Mitsukue, and Kajimachi is hardly a peaceful place. So why did you have to go snooping around my home in particular?
Because of the kaidan stories.
Are you or are you not involved with those kaidan stories? Tell me.
'Simply appearing at the same time that a few strange events occur' and 'being the one who spawned the haunted house and the kaidan stories' are two very different things, alright?
If I could spawn kaidan stories all by myself, what would I be doing riding a unicycle all over the place?
So basically, you were just tagging along for fun, but people mistook you for the kaidan from the legend?
Not bad. You're pretty sharp.
I'm telling you, being a kami is a job that depends entirely on the whims of fate.

This Kizuna Jinja is about bringing people together. Most of you spend your whole lives dealing with other people, and there's probably nothing more important than the bonds you form, don't you think?
When times are stable, people pray for their existing bonds to last. When times are turbulent, they pray for these bonds to carry them through—or for new ones to form.
But over the past decade or so, fewer and fewer people have come here. These days, on our best days, we only get a dozen or so visitors.
Is this true, Kannushi-sama?
It's exaggerating a bit, but we indeed have been getting far fewer visitors the past few years.
Some kami are lazy. The fewer visitors they get, the happier they are. I'm not like that. If it's always this slow, I'm going to die of boredom, you know?
Speaking of lazy.
What?
There's a lazy one right at your feet.
Hoshiguma looks down at her feet, but there is nothing there, only the stone floor of the shrine.
Hey, show yourself, you're the shrine's star, no?
Hoshiguma reaches down to feel around her feet.
The priest can see that Hogishuma's hand is touching the center of the kami's body, yet Hoshiguma doesn't even realize that she's touched anything at all.
Here's the reason our shrine is known for forging bonds. Whenever he feels someone has been having good luck in love or relationships, it'll show itself.
But if it thinks you haven't had much luck with relationships lately, or that you're about to have a run of terrible luck, it'll flat-out refuse to show itself.
I've said all of that, and yet it still doesn't want to appear before you. It sounds like you're in for some bad luck.
Your actions will determine your future. You don't have to take Its words at face value, Hoshiguma-san.
Why are you speaking against your own kami?
Basically, when people get bored, they tend to pursue random aims. Kami are no different.
Right after you disappeared, the kaidan tales started to spread through Mitsukue. There's nothing wrong with chasing a few kaidan to pass the time, is there?
Why the silent treatment? Do you fear your imminent misfortune?
Not really. I just didn't expect that a kami would chase after something so fleeting and unreal.
Why you little!
Regardless, you are not the kami Moriuchi is looking for.
Of course not.
But, might I trouble you to come travel with us? Talk is cheap. If you could just chat with him the way you're chatting with me and Momoka for a bit...
Kannushi-sama, help! This Oni wants to steal away your beloved kami!
These past few years, Moriuchi has given me many gifts, including a never-ending supply of oden.
Hmph.
Forget it. I'm a softy, after all. I'll go with you.
Hoshiguma, have you finished your chat with the priest?
We're almost finished. Where is Kichisei?
She left already. She had to go back and mind the store.
Is this girl coming with us?
Yes, Momoka is coming too. She knows all the kaidan stories. That should be enough to convince you to tag along, yeah?
I already told you I was going. I didn't need more convincing, so why does it matter that she's coming with?
Despite its words, the kami still pedals its unicycle over to Momoka, sniffing to the left and peeking over to the right, looking thoroughly intrigued.
Little girl, I've seen so many of your TV shows. Have you been afflicted with some kind of curse?
H-How should I know?!
I'm actually afraid of kaidan. I just want a quiet life, without all the weird stuff.
You're not the least bit curious about them?
I just want to advance my entertainment career.
The kaidan stuff helped me broaden my audience, but I don't really feel... that's the best way to do it.
Entertainers should rely on their own talent to bring people joy. That's how it should be done.
There aren't too many people who still think like you do. Interesting.
By the way, I wanted to ask you something! Last night, some really scary stuff happened in my house!
Out of nowhere, my TV started controlling itself; it showed clips of me singing, cut together with those blaring emergency alerts.
Then at the end, I have no idea why, but it played a really... scary video.
Tsk tsk tsk! What was the video about? Some cryptid? A bloody murder scene?
None of those things. It was... a video of Mio-san. And soon after the video ended, she actually did show up at my place...
Wasn't that just your TV on the fritz? That's not the kind of kaidan I'm looking for.
It's not?
You've been studying these kaidan. Don't you think they're actually just humans messing around?
You really don't get it, do you? If the ghost stories were all just human mischief, would I even be investigating them in the first place?
Each of these kaidan defy any conceivable explanation.
When people can't figure out the cause of something, guys like Moriuchi just go and pin the blame on us kami.

And if even a kami can't figure out what's going on, well, that just means there's someone or something stronger, more mysterious, and way more thrilling than me pulling the strings behind the scenes.
But you're the one riding the unicycle.
Ahem... That doesn't matter. Don't try to change the subject!
So you mean you're also chasing kami? You're chasing the 'kami above the kami'?
Exactly!
Kichisei?
You swapped terminal numbers with Kichisei?
Yes. The agency doesn't usually let me interact with my fans. And she's such a big one.
Hello? Kichisei?
What?
What?!
That's not true! I didn't mean it—that's not what I meant!
Right! I never once said I was running!
Yes. Uhuh.
You can't talk now...? You think you're being followed?!
Okay. Well then I'll...
Oh? The shrine too?
Right. I'll tell everyone right away. Be careful!
Someone spread word that you're running away?
Kichisei said it was spreading all over the forums. It didn't seem like some random rumor, more like something Kinseki Channel deliberately leaked…
What's up with Kichisei then?
A Kinsekikai guy was tailing her. She also said the area around the shrine might not be safe, she saw people sneaking around when she stepped out through the shrine gate.
The next time you see Kichisei, please thank her for me.
You really don't need to be too worried. Whether or not the yakuza are running around Mitsukue, even if they dare invade the shrine, they're just causing themselves trouble.
If you need to take shelter within the shrine grounds, you can head inside. I'll handle everything else. If you'd rather leave, I'll show you the way out. Take the side path.
I think we'd better leave. The longer we stay here, the more men they can deploy around here to get us.
Momoka, you're gonna have to ride the scooter.
Okay! I've been riding all night. I think I'm starting to get the hang of it!
Then we'll be going, Kannushi-sama.
If I go with you, are those yakuza going to chase me too?
We'll try our hardest not to attract any attention—
Then I'll be the one providing cover?
By 'cover,' you mean—
You're going to help us draw away the yakuza, right?
That's pretty much the idea. Playing those slow-witted yakuza fools every now and then, well, it's not as thrilling as chasing kaidan, but it does pass the time.
I'll make a break through the front gate first to distract the dumb yakuza goons. You all slip out the back and we'll rendezvous at that moron's yatai.
Before anyone even has the chance to respond, the unicycle shoots through the shrine gate like lightning, its pedals spinning so fast they're just a blur.
It seems like that kami really cares about you.
Haha...
But now's not the time for sentimentality. You two come over here. I'll show you the way.
This path is pretty well-hidden...
Yes, but that's not to say that no one will find it. Get out of here as quickly as you can.
Thank you for your help and generosity.
Don't mention it—
There's something moving over this way! Come on! Bring your camera over here! I think there's someone here!
This area is still part of the shrine grounds. Are you sure it's okay for us to barge in here?
What matters more: Mifune-san's orders, or some dumb shrine rules? Get over here!
You don't need to start throwing hands like a street thug! You just need a mic and a camera, and you're set. Can you think of an easier gig?! Get over here!
Kannushi-sama, wh—what do we do now?!
The kannushi says nothing. The easygoing look he's worn—with an occasional hint of self-satisfaction—is nowhere to be found.
You two, please wait here for a moment.
You're going to go fight them?
Just the usual routine, clearing away a bit of lingering evil
The priest gives the gohei a casual flick. The wind chimes fall silent as the leaves quiver. The air suddenly turns thin and clear.

Gleaming fire gushes from the wide sleeves of his robe, like streams of spring water—neither surging nor even trembling—only drifting quietly through the air, until a gust of wind sweeps through from the shrine gate.
It is a cruel wind, sharp as a knife, the kind no kami would raise in idle sport with humankind.

Seeing the faint foxfire flare up in the rear courtyard, the kami at the shrine gate lets go of the handlebars and rises to its feet. With a trace of pride, it lifts a forepaw and sweeps it through the air—
The crystalline flames burst like exorcising thunder, and the shimenawa hung around the shrine instantly ignite with foxfire, severing the mortal world from the divine realm.
The fierce wind erupts along the edge of the divine realm, tearing away every camera, drone, and listening device, along with their camouflage, and hurling them into the air.
Woah! That's some wind! Why is all the equipment flying all over the place?!

The gohei slices into the wind and shears apart the surging foxfire. The wind strips the casings off the surveillance equipment, while the flames shoot through each newly exposed core with perfect precision.
The surveillance equipment ignites with brilliant, silent white flames; a moment later, bursts apart and falls to the ground as bits of scorched charcoal.
Kannushi-sama...? Are you actually an Arts master?
Just a few little tricks, is all. Nothing spectacular.
Now's your chance. Get going!
Hoshiguma, and Momoka?
Did you need something to tide you over for now? Or do you mean to tell me you've already completed your task?
I... I don't need anything to eat... I'm pretty full. I feel like I swallowed enough wind on the way over here...
The Kinsekikai are still chasing you?
Thanks to the kami-sama's guidance, and by taking a less-traveled route, the journey back went relatively smoothly.
K-Kami-sama... you mean?!
Don't celebrate just yet. Yes, we found a kami-sama, but not the one you were looking for.
That doesn't matter. It's not a problem!
It doesn't matter?
You, you're...?
See? I told you.

No, no, no! I don't have anything here to offer you a proper welcome... but how about some oden broth to warm you—
No. Have all the oden you want!
I'm not as into the oden you make as certain others.
Kami-sama, I wonder if you—
Wonder what? You're wondering if I've seen the maneki-neko you keep talking about, right? The neko's the one you're looking for, not me.
No! Getting to meet a kami is a blessing from heaven. Why would I ever be picky?
Your eyes betray you.
Back when that neko would still talk to me, it talked to me about you, and the one it described was nothing like you here. You were just a kid then, treating it the way any kid would treat a friend.
That is why It left me?
How should I know? It remembers you, but just as one of the thousands of people it's met in its life. It would never waste its time telling me why it 'left you.'
Err...
I came to see you for other reasons.
No problem! What do you need from me? Money, or information?
Information, of course, information about the kaidan.
You think we kami are responsible for all of the kaidan in Mitsukue, but I promise you... we have basically nothing to do with any of them.
Aren't you, in fact, the Wanyuudou from the legend?
I admit that I speed my unicycle down the street every day, but what else makes you think I'm the Wanyuudou?
The old building behind the police station on Doku Street. The 'cursed elevator'.
The building only has fifteen floors, yet the elevator has a button for the sixteenth floor. That extra floor is actually 'hell'. People have disappeared there.
Sometimes the maneki-neko in the lobby, which is normally fixed to the front desk, just vanishes, only to reappear the next day. This seems to be correlated to when the sixteenth floor appears.
The phenomenon isn't limited to the old building. Many others have told me that their maneki-nekos disappear and reappear at odd times.
Speaking specifically about the old building though, every time the 'entrance to hell' thing happens, the maneki-neko disappears.
You have some pretty detailed knowledge there, but I can tell you for sure that the maneki-neko in that building has nothing to do with the 'kami' you're looking for, even if they look alike.
Where's your proof?
Why do I need proof? I'm a kami. Shouldn't I be able to discern between kami and look-a-likes?
You're just talkin'. You haven't proved anything.
Where's all the deference you had just a minute ago?
Well...
I'm not calling you out or blaming you. I like the frankness better than the whole show you were putting on before.
Besides, I've also explored that old building pretty carefully.
First, there's no hidden button. The button for the sixteenth floor is right above the button for the fifteenth floor. It's just covered by a steel plate.
Kami didn't do that, and no human could lift that steel plate.
So you're saying the button for the sixteenth floor has been there all along, just covered by a steel plate?!
A pale look comes over Moriuchi's face.
See, even you think it's silly, right?
Second, that maneki-neko can actually be pressed down. If you push hard enough, the whole thing sinks below the counter.
Did you... push it?
Yeah, I pushed it and nothing amazing happened. When the receptionist came back, he fiddled with the desk a bit, and the thing popped back up.
Then the receptionist started answering the phone, a long string of calls, and kept apologizing and stuff. That was it.
See, what we have here is a quirky receptionist, but you're saying it's a kaidan.
So that's just a weird habit of his? How can that be?
Well, what else could it be? You think he's pushing the maneki-neko below the desk because he has a grudge against that neko?
Um...
What is it, girl? Have you filmed a show in that building or something?
No. But when a variety show came to film here, one of my coworkers floated that idea, only to get an earful from the producer at our agency.
He chewed him out?

The producer went on about a bunch of stuff I barely understood, something about a 'Tekkaba' being a Kinsekikai business. I guess it's some kind of restaurant, and they didn't want a TV show hurting their business.
Tekkaba?
Tekkaba?!
Yes. What's wrong? 'Tekka' is the name of a Higashinese finbeast dish, so wouldn't 'Tekkaba' just be a restaurant that sells 'Tekka'?
'Tekkaba' is a code word for 'casino'.
So I guess 'hell' is just a Kinsekikai casino, and anyone who wanders in there by mistake is made to keep their mouth shut.
The button for the sixteenth floor has to be triggered by some kind of mechanism. It probably leads to a hidden mezzanine—or maybe even a basement.
That maneki-neko is probably some kind of signaling device. Press it, and it sends a message. That's why after our dear kami-sama pressed it, the receptionist apologized to them.
Momoka lets out a sigh. It's hard to tell whether it's out of pity for Moriuchi or out of relief that there isn't a real kaidan.
The Nosy Kami scratches Its head in mild irritation. It clearly wasn't expecting the kaidan tale to end like this.
Moriuchi's face grows ever more pale, until the sweat flows down from his eyebrows.
That must just be a coincidence...
After all these years... some kaidan tales have been debunked, but there are still so many no one can explain! The sixteenth-floor 'hell' thing must just be a coincidence!
There's also the 'last remaining Fukui-ke diner.' I remember the kami mentioning to me that It liked that place, back when I still kept in touch with It.

After the restaurant was torn down a couple of years ago, people would sometimes say they caught the scent of fried food drifting through the area. Other people even claimed they could sometimes see the diner still standing on the empty lot. Only they could never get close to it.
That lot has been empty ever since the restaurant was torn down.

I remember that place. I wanted to go try it, back when it was still around, but the owner wouldn't let me in, and I ended up going somewhere else. The restaurant closed not long after the TV show aired.

For the next few days after that, rumors were flying that I cursed the restaurant's owner. People would say 'the Kaidan Queen is at it again' or 'the spiteful woman took her revenge,' stuff like that...
The Kinsekikai are the ones who tore it down.
Obviously I know that! But we're talking about what happened after it got torn down. Momoka, didn't you and your film crew visit the lot after that?
Y-Yes, but... when we went there, I didn't see any restaurant. I smelled some fried food, but that was it.
Anyone can easily make a place smell like fried food. Take the kaidan out of it, and this thing boils down to the Kinsekikai wanting to force the Fuku family out of business, right?
First they planted the idea that something spooky might be going on here. Then they tore the place down. Finally, they sent Momoka over there for one last visit.
But...but...the Ao-Oni! The Haunted House!
Moriuchi-san, are you really going to stand here and talk to me about that?
No offense, but if you didn't cause the deaths that happened in that house, then it could still have been the work of a kami!
Even this kami-sama only started chasing kaidan after the Haunted House rumor took off, right?
That is true.
Momoka, didn't you just recently film a TV show at the Haunted House?
Ahh... ahaha... yes, but it wasn't a ghost who scared me, it was Hoshiguma. I tried to pick something off the floor to hit her with, and—
'Don't forget—out of a house full of trash, she just had to pick up that broken blade. We searched everywhere and couldn't find it, so we had no choice but to hastily forge a fake one.'
'Even if she didn't do it intentionally, that changed everything.'
Momoka?
No, no, no, no...
Though she may be saying 'no', Momoka, hands shaking, pulls the broken blade out.
Hoshiguma, this is just a broken blade, right? Mifune-san wouldn't kill me over this thing, right?
Broken blade?
I picked it up at your place the day we filmed the show. Then, last night, when my TV went haywire in the middle of the night, I thought I heard Mifune-san say it was because I picked up this blade…
Look at this blade guard. This is from the Sanoguchis' heirloom blade, isn't it? Sanoguchi used to love showing it off.
Sanoguchi? The second victim in the Haunted House, the one who used his family blade to kill his wife and then take his own life?
But more than one police officer told me that blade has been locked up in the evidence room. How could it be sitting in your family's old house?
Mifune-san must want that blade, and so he gave the police some false evidence, right?
Then why isn't this blade in his collection? Why would it end up in my house, and broken in two?

Besides, when a gangster goes mad and kills himself, the yakuza practically welcome the cops to investigate, just to watch them come up empty and leave in frustration. There's no reason for them to forge evidence.
Unless Sanoguchi didn't kill himself. Unless Kinbee...
...Murdered him.
Momoka stands there, stunned.
She stares at Hoshiguma. The expression on Momoka's face is even more blank and stunned than Moriuchi's. It's as if she's awakening from one nightmare, only to step into another one.
You're joking, right?
Those kaidan tales… the ones that scared me half to death… how could they not be real?
The agency might be shameless, but there's no way that all these years, they've been fabricating… all… sorts… of…
And... Sanoguchi... his death was suspicious... but the others...
Her voice grows softer and softer... then stops.
She subconsciously looks to Moriuchi, who intentionally avoids making eye contact.
She suddenly realizes that she has been looking at Moriuchi because she thought he shared her pain, while he averted his gaze because he didn't want to admit the truth.
So all those businesses that closed after I visited them...
Those livestreams where I did as I was told, and weird things kept happening where people got hurt...
Those eerie noises, the ghosts, the fleeting shadows... Were they… all fake?

Even the Ao-Oni and the Haunted House, the legends I believed so completely, the ones that scared me out of my wits these past few days, the starting point of all the tales in Mitsukue… were they all… fake?
Momoka Hanyuu, the singer wrapped up in kaidan tales, my agency never even cared about my career?
To them, I've been nothing more than a... cover for the Kinsekikai?
That can't be right. Can it, Moriuchi-san?
Moriuchi-san...?
It's an ambush! Get behind me!
Momoka is still frozen in place. Hoshiguma steps forward, positioning the Hannya mask between her and the collapsed Moriuchi, shielding them both.
As if in answer to Momoka's question, the surface of the shield crackles with a flurry of tiny explosions.
Kami-sama, check on Moriuchi. Was he poisoned or struck by something?
There's a burn mark on his neck, and this thing here fell on the floor.
Isn't this a flash drive you use to move data between terminals?
I've seen people throwing these things at electronic equipment to disable it, but lately, they've been throwing them at people instead. Why?
Ninja?!
Don't try to resist.
Who sent you?
That's not important. Come with me.
There's no way Mifune-san sent you. Right?
That's the first time I've heard anyone ask that question.
Please tell me! The kaidan tales are all true! My career isn't being used as a cover for Mifune... errr... Kinbee!
The ninja replies only with a weapon, which looks somewhat like a blade but different. The blade has yet to move, but remains pointed squarely at Hoshiguma's chest.
Spies, schemers, arsonists, even assassins—people who spend their entire lives in the shadows, never daring to show their true faces in public.
Since the national wars several centuries ago, these people, who call themselves 'ninja', have moved through the hidden currents of history.
Since then, countless figures of power: daimyo, kugyou, zaibatsu magnates, clan leaders have all assembled their own shadowy organizations.
Wuh?!
You hid yourself well, but you're not quick enough on the draw.
Hoshiguma plants a foot on the flash drive she's just blocked. It gives a sharp electronic whine, then bursts apart with a loud pop.
Keeping up with the times, huh? A flash drive with a high-voltage charge. Works on both people and machines?
The two ninjas exchange a glance. One draws a short blade, the other a long sword, and together they lunge at Momoka from opposite sides.
You can't leave well enough alone!

Hannya spins in Hoshiguma's hands, a blazing circle of fury. The long sword's edge strikes it and buckles, shattering to pieces. The ninja with the dagger dives to the ground, dodging the whirling shield before it can shred limbs.
Only then does the third figure, hidden all this time in the shadows, appear—sending a cluster of flash drives silently hurtling toward Hoshiguma's unprotected head.
It's too late to call out a warning, so Momoka leaps to her feet, trying to imitate a movie scene. She swings her shoulder bag in an attempt to bat the little drives back.
Momoka takes a direct hit.
There is a faint crackling sound. She doesn't even have time to cry out before she crumples to the floor, limp as a rag, just like Moriuchi.
Gah!
Momoka!
You're the last one standing. You either come with me, quietly, or you end up on the floor like them.
Overestimating your own abilities is never a good thing.
Enough talk. Have at you!
The projectiles come from every direction, expected and otherwise, only to shatter against the whirling Hannya.
She's trying to run while carrying the other two on her back. Stop her!
Hoshiguma charges the nearest ninja head-on. The black-clad assassin slams into the shield, snapping many ribs.
She is about to break through the encirclement when her shoulder suddenly feels light. Something has yanked Moriuchi up into the air.
A hook?!
Moriuchi is still dangling in midair when Momoka is snatched up, like a doll caught in a crane machine.

Hannya slices through the rope attached to the grappling hook. Momoka drops safely into Hoshiguma's arms, while the oden yatai owner is flung by the momentum and lands in a heap in front of his own cart.
One of the ninjas seizes the oden man, leaps into the air, and vanishes among the buildings of Nita Street.
H-Hoshiguma...
You came to pretty quickly.
My neck really hurts...
Looks like some scorch marks. I think you got zapped. Rinse it and cover it, and it shouldn't leave a scar.
There might be more attackers coming in from Kajimachi. Do you have somewhere we can lie low for a bit?
My place, but I bet Kinbee's already sent guys over there...
Doesn't matter. We'll hide in plain sight. They won't expect us to go there. And if someone is there, we'll just go somewhere else.
Perfect. That way, I can bring Hasuke along too…
Hasuke?
Never mind, I'll explain later. The longer we stall, the more goons will show up. Which way to your place? Let's go.
Kami-sami, you—
...Are already gone.
Put me down, I can walk on my own.
Alright.
Wh-What happened?
My room, it's a complete mess... have Kinbee's guys been here already?
Careful, someone might be lying in ambush—
Momoka ignores Hoshiguma's warning and runs towards the window.
Ha...
...suke?
H-How could this happen...?
Who cut my Hasuke?
Cut?
Following Momoka to the window, Hoshiguma sees the objects lined up on it, and...
A flowerpot.
There is only the stem of a plant left; the upper half has been severed.
Hoshiguma turns her head towards the trash can, and sees the upper half lying inside.
Hasuke is a tree?
A sapling! It didn't grow much because I haven't taken good care of it.
Who did this? And why? Why? Why?!
Momoka, calm down—
Let me go! I'll get revenge for Hasuke!
For some reason, Momoka seems to get angrier with each word that Hoshiguma says.
Her eyes are wide. She is panting hard, and trembling all over, seemingly willing to fight to the death.
Hoshiguma instinctively takes a step back and even has the urge to gulp, something that she does not often do. Not that Momoka is trying to do anything to her, but...
Momoka intends to kill. Hoshiguma can sense it.
Hoshiguma takes another step back and touches a water bottle. She immediately pours a cup of water and hands it to Momoka.
Here, have some water.
Momoka does not answer. She snatches the cup out of Hoshiguma's hands and empties it in a single gulp, leaving bite marks on the edge of the paper cup.
At least it seems to have calmed her down a little... if only a little.
It must have been Kinbee's people who did this to Hasuke, right?
Very likely.
But... why?
Did they cut Hasuke to vent their anger just because I wasn't home? How could they do this?
Judging from what I see in the trash can, Hasuke wasn't in very good shape—
Momoka takes a big step towards Hoshiguma. Hoshiguma almost knocks over the water bottle as she flinches.
Of course Hasuke's in bad shape! How could it be anything else, when all my time is occupied with work?
Huh?
Of course I wanted Hasuke to grow up healthy and keep changing its pots until it's too big for my room!
But I have to do overtime when the agency asks. I don't get home until 3 or 4 in the morning, and a lot of times I don't even remember when I last watered it.
But that's okay. I could take notes and measure the water.
But the people at the agency laugh at me when I say I want to take Hasuke with me on long trips!
I tried those blogger tips and put eyedrop vials in the soil, but it doesn't work. Hasuke's leaves are always yellow when I come back from a long trip, and it almost wilted a few times!
I had to work hard to save it every time. How could it grow when it's always on the brink of death?
It's very important to you. Is it...?
The question that came to Hoshiguma's mind was 'a rare species', but she immediately realizes that it is not what she should ask.
Is it... a gift from someone dear to you?
No, I picked it up from the roadside.
The roadside?
I came to Mitsukue with Hanamaru, a cloudbeast I had kept for a long time.
As my work got busier, I no longer had time to care for her, so I gave her away along with her climbing rack. I bought a similar one to remember her.
I knew I didn't have time to keep a pet, so I thought I would keep a plant.
And that was Hasuke?
No, my first one was Ran.
I tried my best to make time to take care of Ran, following the instructions in the manual to the letter, but after a few months...
Hoshiguma glances at the flowerpot next to the cloudbeast climbing rack.
A little seedling sprouted in Ran's pot. I asked the flower shop owner and he said it was a berry seedling, and suggested that I changed pots. That was Ichigo.
Then the windows of my room broke on a windy day, and by the time I got back home...
Ran, Ichigo, Matsumaru, Toge-dono... my plants always die in strange ways. Toge-dono died because of a flood upstairs!
I started suspecting that plants and I just weren't good together, but I couldn't keep a pet because I might have to go on a long trip at a moment's notice at any time.
But I would come home to an empty room, without even a single blade of grass I could talk to without putting on a mask... I couldn't take it anymore, which was why I got Tamasaburou.
This electronic pet? What happened to Tamasaburou?
When I talked to the repair shop owner, he said it left the factory with a defective motherboard.
I picked up Hasuke with its pot from a trash dump on my way back from the repair shop.
When I found Hasuke, I made up my mind... I would give up on keeping a plant if I couldn't keep it alive.

With tears in her eyes, Momoka walks from one end of the window sill to the other, showing them to Hoshiguma one by one. Hoshiguma is not sure whether to laugh or cry, until Momoka pulls the last bag open.
It is not a memorial for a pet or plant, but two memorial tablets. The names inscribed upon them are 'Kazuhiko Shino' and 'Kumiko Shino'.
What is this?
My Mama and Papa.
You don't mean—
No, no, it's not that.
I grew up in Gokawa, near the southern border.
It was a small backwater nomadic city, in Kaneshiro territory. It was known for its plastics and chemical plants, and nothing else.
Waste from the factories had polluted the water supply. People didn't know they were drinking polluted water until a few years before I was born.
I managed to grow up pretty healthy, but my parents were not so lucky.
Papa died when I was very little, around five or six years old. I was brought up by my mother.
I don't remember much about Papa. As for Mama... she was never in good health. She complained about not being able to see clearly, feeling numb in her hands and feet, and was also prone to falling.
The Kaneshiro clan hushed things up, giving people with clear health problems a pittance, while finding all sort of excuses to deny relief to people who could still take care of themselves.
Mama's condition had really deteriorated by the time Kinbee's agency scouted me, partly by persuasion and partly by coercion. She passed away not long after my debut.
I wanted to attend her funeral, but the agency wouldn't let me, because I had become somewhat well-known by then, and they didn't want people to know that I came from Gokawa.
They gave me the surname name 'Hanyuu', which sounds northern, because the surname 'Shino' might clue people in on where I came from.
My relatives took care of the funeral, but the agency prohibited me from telling them the true reason why I couldn't go back.
They cut ties with me after sending Mama's spirit tablet and ashes to me.
The agency told me the best way to comfort my parents' souls was to become a star.
Like force-feeding a fowlbeast, they kept telling me to become the biggest star in Mitsukue, in Higashi, on Terra, that it was the meaning of my entire existence.
And so I kept walking down this path, forcing myself to believe that they were right, until a few days ago.

My performing career was the entirety of my life. Anything that could help my career, I had to do, no matter how unwilling I was. Anything that could hurt my career, I couldn't touch, no matter how attractive it was.
But... I'm tired. I would be, even without all those strange kaidan that keep me on edge every day.
It's not that I don't like my work, but... who can I talk to about things other than work?
So... this is what it's come to. Nothing I kept ever lived long, their memories forming a long row on my window sill...
Silly, isn't it? A girl who kills anything she tries to keep.
Momoka Hanyuu should go home alone, take off her makeup, shower, brush her teeth, and go straight to bed, then straight to work the next day when she wakes up...
Momoka Hanyuu should do exactly as her agency says and stop trying to have a life outside of her performing career.
But Hasuke lived for a long time, even though I never took good care of it. But now... now!
Momoka—
Sorry, what's your real name?
Haruka.
Mama and Papa gave me the name because they wanted me to go far away. Away from Gokawa, away from the dirty water that killed without taste or smell, to live in freedom.
I made it far away, alright. As for living in freedom...
Should I call you...?
No, don't. Call me Momoka. Momoka Hanyuu.
Sorry.
It's not that I don't like the name my parents gave me... how could I not like it?
But... I've come too far. I don't have a choice anymore.
Haruka Shino has no parents, no relatives, no friend who knows the name. A breeze... a breeze is all it takes to blow her away.
All I am is Momoka Hanyuu, the performer.
Even if the agency doesn't want me anymore, even if Kinbee wants me to get Oripathy, even if he's trying to silent me, Momoka Hanyuu is the only thing I am, and the only thing I can be.
Silence.
Then... pack up, Momoka. We're leaving.
Kinbee's ninjas could come back at any time. We need to go to Kajimachi.
Yeah.
The Ægir wipes her tears and starts packing silently.
Then she looks up at Hoshiguma, forcing a smile on her tear-stained face.

Say, can we go and see the fireworks tomorrow? I've been living in Mitsukue for so many years, but I've never had the chance... never had the time to go.
I used to tell Mio-san that I would go to the riverside and watch the fireworks if I ever lost my job. Now, it looks like...
Well...
She never finishes the sentence as her smile gradually fades, as though she knows that it is a silly question, and is just waiting for someone to interrupt.
Hoshiguma cannot find the words to console her.
Bring it on, bakayaro!
I'll pay you double what that gomikuzu Kinbee is paying to cut off that white tail of his! I wanted a belt to go with this outfit anyway—
Stop throwing this crap! What's in it? Kinbee's dirty photos or something? How much do you have to hate it to throw stuff like this?
Watch your tongue.
That's what I was waiting for!
A jam?! Are you kidding me?
Ouch!
My hand... I can't move it...
What do those famous people say in their death poems? 'In the fifty years of human life'? Well I haven't lived that long...
Shut it. I was just planning to take you to the oyabun, but it looks like Mitsukue would be a better place without you.
Ha, now ain't that an honor...
Shut up and accept your death, lest your spirit become a ghost that shall not know rest—
What's that?!
My blade! It was broken by a shogi piece?!
Careful, Tessai! They mean business!
Take this, old bastard!
The ninja yells at Tessai, but throws the broken sword to Kichisei lying on the ground.
Kichisei sees Tessai's movements clearly for the first time.
His iron fan pulls in the broken blade like a magnet. Tessai swings the fan, and the blade flies from the fan—
Gugh...
Th-Thanks, Old Man... you saved my skin...
You want to sleep, do it in a bed. You still have to mind the parlor tomorrow.
Alright...
Footsteps! Are there more of them?
Hoshiguma... Momoka too! Are you alright?
Were you crying, Momoka?
I'm fine.
Kichisei, you...?
Would be dead if the Old Man didn't show up.
Uncle Tessai, Momoka's being hunted by Kinbee's people and has nowhere to go. I want to hide her in Kajimachi for a while...
No problem! Plenty of space in the Old Man's pachinko parlor—
Kichisei takes one glance at Tessai and realizes that he is not exactly in a great mood.
What I mean is, she can stay upstairs in my shop!
Hmph, suit yourself.
Thanks, Old Man! I'll take her—
One moment. Do you really plan on parading her through the town as though that son of mine doesn't exist?
You mean... Tetsuya?
He's been snooping around. The girl can stay at my place until he leaves.
...He won't go there.
Well, let's go home—
Tetsuya?!
The Lucky Star ain't open today. What are you trying to do, barging in while I'm out?
Er, I...
I'm sorry! I was looking for the Northern Court spy, and I haven't searched your home yet, so...
I'm the only one here. Do I look like a spy to you?
Wait, how did you get hurt? Was it the Northern Court—
Why would a Northern Court spy attack me? Just got in a little fight, that's all. All I need is a good night's sleep.
Er, how about I bandage it up for you?
I almost forgot. You started studying medicine before working for Kinbee, and wanted to try your skills on anyone in town who had a bump or bruise. Must've read too many swordfight manga...
Fine, suit yourself. Let's see if your skills have improved.
Who did this to you? It almost went all the way through.
Would you believe me if I said it was Kinbee?
Don't talk bad about Mifune-san, Kichisei. He's doing it for the greater good.
Forget the greater good, I remember how YOU were crying about how you were gonna become a swordmaster and clear your mom's name.
What happened to that with the development of Kajimachi? Did the window displays in the luxury shops eat up all your time?
I left. I didn't have to live off compensation money like you all, and I didn't want you to think that I was working for him out of greed.
What kind of bloody baka are you?
I'm not kidding, it was Kinbee's men who did this to me! I would be dead if your pop didn't step in!
—What?
Ouch!
Sorry! But—no, why would Mifune-san want to kill you? It must be a misunderstanding!
Misunderstanding? I saw Hoshiguma once at the shrine, and then his men started tailing me. You tell me where the misunderstanding is!
I-I'll go and find him! I'll smooth things over, if he's really angry at you for some reason.
Are you his son?
You're beyond saving. Seriously, I want to introduce the muzzle of my gun to that dumbass mouth of yours.
Now get lost, before I actually do it. I'll wrap the rest of the wounds myself.
...What a loser.
So that's the truth behind the kaidan?
What about your family home? Shimasu, Sanoguchi... that bastard Kinbee killed all of them?
Strictly speaking, I only have proof for Sanoguchi.
Do they have evidence, then, Moriuchi, Momoka, and that so-called kami?
Do you have proof for the Diet candidate dying by your hand twenty years ago?
I could never forget what happened that night.
You came back with blood all over you, standing at the door of this very manor, not saying a single word.
I thought you had failed to stop Kinbee, but you told me that the Diet was dead, and you killed him.
You accidentally killed Shuuji, whom you were close to, while trying to stop Kinbee. Then you went berserk and started killing indiscriminately...
The Diet candidate was dead by the time you came to your senses.
Who can prove that you did it? Only yourself, when you don't even remember what happened?
If you think that Mifune didn't kill Shimasu and the others, what makes you say that you killed the Diet candidate?
Well...
There was a succession crisis brewing, and you did, indeed, kill brothers of the clan—not one, but several.
You thought the people wouldn't accept someone who would kill her own brothers. Indeed, you couldn't accept it yourself.
The Diet candidate was dead, and my relationship with you was much better than with Kinbee. Placing the blame on you would prove that I was fair and unbiased, at least.

Kinbee was a valued administrator who was conducting the bulk of Kinsekikai's transformation. He put in honest work back then, and was more valuable than someone like you, who only contributed to fighting.
All of these were things that you said to me the next day, when your reason had fully returned. Your arguments were valid, for the greater good, and impossible to ignore at the time.
Did any of it have to do with who killed the Diet candidate, though?
I wanted a straight answer. Did you kill the Diet candidate by the name of Koretou? Yes or no?
You said... 'Yes'.
The rest was history... I pinned the blame on you, then smuggled you to Lungmen. To everyone else, you had gone missing, having likely committed suicide because you feared punishment for the murder.
But one lie must be covered up by another lie. If you had killed the Diet candidate, then Kinbee and his people must have gone to protect him... things snowballed from there.
Let me ask you again, Hoshiguma: Did you kill the candidate?
If the answer is yes, then there is nothing else to say. I did my best after you committed the deed, and it is my fault that the Kinsekikai has become what it is today.
If not...
Tessai says nothing more, but gazes straight into Hoshiguma's eyes.
...The only thing I hoped when I came back was that my sacrifice was not in vain, and that things had gotten better for the Kinsekikai.
But I was wrong.
So you didn't kill him?
No, I...
I don't know.
I remember nothing from when I accidentally killed Shuuji until I saw the candidate's lifeless body. All I know is that I had gone on a rampage.
Then why did you say 'Yes'?
Hoshiguma says nothing and looks in the direction of her family home.
Because of your father?!
What are you doing here, Papa?
Wh-Why are there so many bad people lying on the ground? Did you kill all of them?
It's alright, Kajimachi is safe. Uncle Tessai's looking for you—
I-Is that Aunt Setsuko? Grandma Kinue, too... did the bad people kill them? Even Tetsuya?!
Let's go, Papa, we need to tell Uncle Tessai—
Wh-Why are you pointing your blade at me?
Uncle Tessai! Something's wrong with Papa—
Papa?!
Yes, your father had killed Tetsuya in his madness. But what do Setsuko and my mother's death have to do with him? Hadn't he been protecting them?
I told you, Tetsuya... not just him, but what happened to your father too, it was all the fault of those wandering Oni! None of this would happened, if not for them!
Besides, since when did I say that you needed to take responsibility for what your father did?
I'm not taking responsibility for him.
All I know is that... I was just like him at the time.
It was either me or Kinbee who killed the candidate. It wouldn't have been anyone else.
But it could have been me.
And if things had gone on, it would have been me, sooner or later.
The aging Oni grabs a shogi piece from the table and smashes it to bits on the ground.
Yes, I was angry at your father, but you've always been like a daughter to me!
Do you know who I was thinking of when I made that Oni face on Hannya? Do you know why I gave you the shield before you left?
It was for you that I made Hannya, so that you wouldn't repeat the mistakes of your father!
That fallen noble house where I bought the materials and craftsmanship once had a splendid collection of legendary weapons.
The stories say that they stole the secret arts of a temple, allowing them to craft weapons that were not only incredibly sharp, but also able to suppress the madness of the Oni.
I didn't know if it was real, but the craftsmanship certainly was, and I made the shield intending for it to be my final work.
But I felt hesitant at the ultimate step.
The handle?
The handle.
According to the laws of the secret art, the part connected to the palm must be polished by the wielder personally, until it fits comfortably in the bare hand.
It was like someone had poured cold water over me... the intention, clearly, was to make one learn self-discipline.
What discipline would it take to polish a piece of metal until it fits comfortably in the hand? There would be no need for any secret art!
So I never gave you the shield, which was why everyone who felt themselves up to it had designs on the clan's leadership.

That's why there were so many internal clashes at the time. High-ranking members looked for any opportunity to take each other out, but it was the rank-and-file who suffered the most, and even the honest folk of Kajimachi became caught in the struggle...
...That's how the events of that night came to be.
Hoshiguma is silent.
Karma, karma!
Right or wrong, things have come too far. There is no way to turn back the clock.
How has Lungmen been?
If life hasn't been kind, take your weapon, and we'll have Kinbee's head, if that's the last thing we do. We're descendants of warriors, and we'll have a warrior's death.
If life has been alright, then leave now, and never think about Kinbee or me again. What happened between your father and I is between the two of us. It's not your fault.
Stop going against your heart for the greater good. It's not your place to do so.
Lungmen...
...has been kind to me.
For the first time tonight, Tessai sees Hoshiguma's gaze meet his.
I'm glad to hear that.
But I'll go with you.
Why?
If this is indeed karma, then I want to clean up the loose ends before I go back to Lungmen.
Very well.
Give Hannya to me.
You've decided how to reforge it?
The reforged Hannya would already have been yours, if you haven't been running around with that Momoka girl.
What's this ribbon?
A gift from my friends in Lungmen. I've been using it.
Take this. It's a step forward that you've learned to make compromises. I wouldn't bet against you, if you faced Chi Xiao once again, wielding the reforged Hannya.
It's a good thing to reforge Hannya, but... there's someone I need to rescue. Kinbee's got Moriuchi.
The information broker who sells oden?
The reforging of Hannya can't wait.
The kaidan were fabricated by Kinbee. The only people who know this are you, me, Momoka... a certain kami, and Moriuchi. Kinbee would never let him walk.
It's partly my fault that he's in danger. I need to help him, fast.
I've got something for you, if you need a weapon.
The old man takes out a dusty box from a corner.
What's in the box?
Your old outfit and sword. I've kept them in decent enough condition.
The one that you wielded that night. Take it.
Unless you mean to tell me that you've not changed one bit these twenty years.
Alright.
I see you haven't lost any of your guts.
I've been meaning to ask. What have you been doing in Lungmen?
Your clothes are a little too plain for a triad, but it doesn't seem like you've been leading a quiet life either. What's going on?
Oh, er, I joined the Lungmen police.
Come again?
I work for the L.G.D., the Lungmen police.
You—pfft, hahahahaha!
No wonder the room stinks of cop!
Are you really going to Kajimachi, Mifune-san?
This is between me, Tessai, and Hoshiguma. I have to put an end to it personally.
If things go according to your plan, Tessai will yield, even without Hannya.
Does the idea of Hoshiguma coming back upset you so?
I don't see the need for it.
True, you would have much more work to do if she came back. I'll tell you this plainly, since you'll someday take over for me.
What do you think is the most important to living well in Mitsukue?
Unity?
No... the most important thing is beauty.
A fine appearance will turn the people's prejudices on their head. You and I, we all benefit from it.
If I kept looking like a thug, they would've kicked me off camera the first time I was on TV. I never could have built Kinseki Channel into what it is today.
It's important that we conduct our business with the proper look, with beauty.
There is an Ursine author—his name escapes me—he says that if a gun appears in the first act of a story...
Then a Lateran must hold it by the third act, at the latest.
I believe there is a Columbian group by the name of Blacksteel that uses guns as well, Mifune-san.
Well, you know what I mean.
Mitsukue is too big, and it has grown too quickly. A lot of times, it's hard for us to tell if we're doing the right thing...

And so, we must make sure that everything we do has a proper beginning and end, that everyone is in their proper place, and the trigger of every gun is pulled by the end. This is the proper appearance. This is beauty.
If I get your meaning... you want to see everything through to the bitter end.
Think of it however you want. Feel free to put my words to the test, once you become the Fourth Chairman of the Kinsekikai.
Right now, though, you should prepare to deal with Hoshiguma.
She is a tough one.
But she is an Oni, and one who has already gone berserk once.
Have you heard of an individual by the name of Qui'lon?
All I know is that he lived a very long time ago, and had something to do with the Oni and the temples.
Setting aside those old warrior stories, it's this legend that Tessai talked about the most.
I don't know if they are true, and they're often contradictory. For example, Tessai has told at least three different versions of how Qui'lon died.
But my favorite version is cruel... and beautiful.
Before he reached nirvana and entered Kaśjñāna, Qui'lon heard the cries of the last Oni when they were forced to become people of Ashihara.
By then, however, Qui'lon no longer had the strength to leave Ashoka, nor wield a sword to save his people, or even keep his fiery blue wrath in check—

The flames of rage took over Qui'lon's soul. He lost all reason, and in his mad rampage destroyed an entire army even as he lay at the gates of death, but the kin whom he was trying to protect were also destroyed by his sword.
It is unknown whether Qui'lon died of exhaustion, or took his own life, or whether he was so heartless that he attained nirvana, fully at peace with himself, in spite of what he did.
I'm not sure why you're telling me the legend of Qui'lon right now.
An Oni is at its frailest when it realizes its own powerlessness. As to whether what follows next is depression or madness...
Our foe has already demonstrated the answer, once.
Now, get some rest. I have a guest to greet.
Mifune-san... I bid you good health.
You're not hurt, are you?
No, no! I haven't slept better in a long while!

Yes, in your line of work, it's important to be of good humor, to smile...
To be inquisitive.
Call Hoshiguma. Tell her that... I have something to discuss.
See, Mifune-san... Hoshiguma helped me a lot, you know...
Helped you discover the truth of the Wanyuudou?
Yes—
But not only that, right?
What was going through your mind when you stood in front of your yatai, at a loss? Thinking about all those years you wasted chasing the kaidan, weren't you?
Now you know it was all fabricated. You were fooled by me and these kaidan stories I made up.
Why... Why did you do it?
None of your business. Just think of it as a stroke of bad luck. But life goes on, after all. So, if you're willing, there's something more concrete you can seize.
Are you trying to bribe me?
Bribe? No. Make the call, and you can keep up your little information broker gig going. Refuse, and you know what the Kinsekikai does to our enemies.
Mifune-san—
Would you like a reminder of how you lost your right eye?
I haven't forgotten. I barged in on one of your negotiations while chasing a kaidan...
I took one eye because you saw what you shouldn't have seen. I let you keep the other because I valued your talent, and as a warning to you.
Now, it's time you showed some gratitude.
Please, give me a few minutes to think—
Mifune slowly draws his blade.
Moriuchi's gaze is fixed upon the blade's tip, as it slowly moves to his own throat.
He gulps, unsure whether to open his mouth or close it—
What's that sound?
The office door is kicked open before Moriuchi can even react.
H-Hoshiguma?!
Go!
But you...
He can't keep me. And I have questions for him.
Moriuchi takes one long look at Hoshiguma and hastily leaves.
You don't seem anxious that I took someone from right under your nose.
Why should I be anxious, when something that I thought would take so much effort suddenly sorted itself out on its own?
Why did you forge that letter to bring me back?
It's complicated. I just told the story to somebody else, and I'm not in the mood to tell it again.
But the Old Man must know the simplest reason, however slow he might be... Hannya.
Hannya?!
You didn't even think of that? Some yakuza! No wonder he made an enemy out of his own son, him and his beloved Ao-Oni of Kinsekikai!
Mio!
I'm afraid your break's over. Stall her.
Alright.
You think your boys can beat Uncle Tessai if you stall me? Keep dreaming.
Why do you think victory is just a question of combat?
What are you planning, then?
You'll see.
Now, I'd better be going.
Hold it right there—
You'll be the one holding it, actually.
Fire, and humming.
The scattered screens continue to stand, unmoving.
They've escorted Mifune-san away. I will be your opponent.
Your blade. Draw it.
No need for the blade if it's you.
Is that so?
With the unpleasant sound of metal against metal, a black ethereal hand appears from behind Mio, leisurely wrapping itself around a white tantou.
Three hands, three blades.
You're strong, but that doesn't mean you have what it takes to get away from me, much less without drawing your blade.
You think the sword's my only weapon?
Before she finishes her sentence, Hoshiguma raises her right leg high and brings it down on the round table beside her, with great force.
The table flies into the air, blocking her opponent's view.
With a powerful leap, Hoshiguma points her sheathed sword at her foe's head—
Three flashes greet her, cutting the table open like a hot knife through butter.
The sheath encounters the black hand, delivering great force—
Hyaaah!
...Impressive power.
Why don't you draw your blade?
None of your business.
You're afraid.
Afraid? Me?
You're afraid that drawing your blade will cause something that you don't want to happen.
Qui'lon's death... I see.
What?
But what you fear will happen, whether you draw your blade or not, it will be your inevitable end. Assuming Mifune-san's predictions are accurate.
I don't really get it.
Despicable.
Me?
No, not you.
Me.
Run. Flee.
Moriuchi does not know how long he has been running. He can feel his own innards shaking and trembling.

Huff... huff... huff...
*cough* *cough*
D-Did I shake them?
You got out on your own?
Who's there?!
Forgot me so soon?
K-Kami-sama?
Don't look at me like that. I'm not here to help you. I don't like the answer about the kaidan that I got at your yatai.
I kind of hate it.
I... I get that.
I had planned on cutting ties with you and the kaidan entirely, perhaps even leave Mitsukue and take a trip to the north.
But someone asked me to do something.
The kannushi? He asked you to find me?
Not him.
Who else could it be, then?
You don't mean—
It asked me to bring you a single message.
'Meet me on your usual rooftop.'
C-Could it be...?
I've delivered the message. It's time to go.
The kami turns around on its unicycle. No sooner has it got on than it sees Moriuchi is already a long way off.
...Is this how you repay him, when he's searched for you for so many years?
You know what, it's none of my business.
Moriuchi runs up the steps.
He is running as hard as he can.
A breeze blows in Moriuchi's face as he opens the door.
Many a sunset has he watched lying on this rooftop, watching the sky turn from red to black, until the stars appear, before their light is overcome by the neon city.
The hour is early, and the sky should not look like dusk, but he pays little heed. Perhaps he considers it a sign of the coming of a kami.
He could not describe his own emotions as he gazes at the sky.
Excitement? Anxiety? Fulfillment? Emptiness? Sadness?
But he lies there, waiting for a guest who may never come...
Until the warm breeze sends him into slumber.
Father... I think there's something behind the Kamidana.
Behave yourself, Tooru! This is a sacred shrine!
But it's really there...
Come, Tooru, it's our turn to pray.
Please, Kami-sama, keep our family safe, and may we repay our debts soon...
There... the debt should be settled, with some money left over even...
We're out of the woods!
Wh-What are you doing here? Are you bored of the shrine?
Kami-sama, arigatou!
Father's mood has gotten a lot better, and mother has had time to take me out.
Can I come and see you again?
—No, I'll definitely come and see you again!
Our shop just opened! It's really pretty. Do you want to come and see it?
I'm sorry, Kami-sama, I've been busy helping out at home and haven't had time to come see you... please, don't be angry and let me see you?
K-Kami-sama? Are you still there? Did you leave? Or... do you not want to see me anymore?
I-I'll treat it like you don't want to see me, but you're still listening to me, okay?
Some things have happened at home. Father may have to go back to manual labor.
Mother's been sewing clothes for other people. Her eyesight's declining.
But I'm sure none of that has anything to do with you. You gave so many blessings to our family. Surely you wouldn't take it all back...
Surely...
Kami-sama, Father...
Father is dead. Overdrinking. The blow was too great for him.
...Where are you?
Please, Kami-sama... where are you?
This haunted house...
It's empty... something's not right!
It's connected to the nomadic city's superstructure?
H-How am I suppose to search this place? This is far more than just one house or Kajimachi. The whole plate might be connected...
No, don't get discouraged. As long as I find that bastard—
Wh-What's this?!
Tetsuya stands dumbfounded, staring at dozens of crates that someone has stacked up here.
He blinks a few times and opens one of them.
It's... weapons?!
Katana, crossbows... grenade launchers...
Is that... the Hoashi emblem? That major Northern Court clan? Is this where they hid their weapons?
He takes down a weapon-filled crate and opens the one underneath it, finding Northern Court standard-issue provisions and medical supplies inside.
He furiously takes the crates down one by one, opening all of them.
All contain military supplies manufactured by the Northern Court, every single one.
Th-The Northern Court weapons that Papa says aren't here? But they are—
You've gone too far, boy.
Who are you?
Oyabun wanted you to look for traces of Northern Court spies. It's enough that you found them. You've made a lot of work opening these up, one-by-one.
...Did you just take my picture?
This is big, after all. Best keep some evidence, to prevent disputes.
Let's go.
Where to?
To see Mifune-san.
W-Wait, you were stalking me?
That's not important. What's important is that you're working for Mifune-san, and so am I.
And take that mask off.
Huh?
You're meeting the oyabun. It's a little disrespectful to have your mask on, isn't it?
For some reason, Tetsuya feels a chill run up his spine, perhaps from the cold wind blowing in from the tunnels, but he has no choice but to comply.
Tessai-san has been inside for quite a while. I'm starting to get worried...
Don't worry about him. The Old Man at his forge is like you singing on stage, Hoshiguma punching someone, or me at a pachinko machine.
Are you really good at pachinko?
Ah—hehe...
Trying to change the subject, Kichisei takes a cookie from the plate in front of her and puts it in her mouth.
Uh oh, I've almost eaten all the cookies.
Why are all the cookies here Ninomiya's classic cystymilk flavor? Is it Tessai-san's favorite?
Tetsuya used to like it.
If Tetsuya realized what kind of man Kinbee was...
He would have realized it a long time ago if he could.
To be fair, Tetsuya following Kinbee around is really just the effect. The root cause goes back to Old Man Tessai—
If only Tessai had half your wisdom, Kichisei-san.
Kinbee?!
Go inside, Momoka! I'll hold him off!
Let's not rush to a fight, kiddo.
My, my. How honored I am, to be welcomed by the oyabun himself.
Where is Hoshiguma?
She is enjoying the hospitality of my people, and is unable to help you.
Despicable scum!
Blame yourself. I was trying to bait her with Moriuchi, but she bit the hook before I even had time to set the bait.
But I don't like to kill unless I have to. These are more civilized times.
I'm here to extend a sincere invitation to Chairman Tessai to attend my accession ceremony.
Your what?
You heard me.

If Chairman Tessai will not hand over Kajimachi whatsoever, then I must take the small liberty of taking over the leadership of the Kinsekikai. After all, the position is already little more than a figurehead.
If you are too embarrassed to attend, you can simply hand over Hannya, and the ceremony can take place without you.
Hannya is not the symbol of succession, never was!
It is if everyone believes it is.
You favored Hoshiguma too much. You never even realized it, did you?
Or did you choose to give Hannya to Hoshiguma before she went to Lungmen for the sole purpose of snubbing me? What did she have that I didn't, that you would detest me so?
You think too highly of yourself.
Yes! That's what I wanted to hear!
It's not that I think too highly of myself, Old Man. You look down on everyone except for your beloved Hoshiguma!
Standing alone at the door, Mifune laughs.
Momoka and Kichisei slowly move back behind Tessai.
Perhaps your son would not have turned against you if I told you this sooner.
Why bring up Tetsuya? Did you think I opposed you in order to pass leadership onto that kid? He's not made for it.
I know. He's another one you look down on.
Enough talk. I may not be able to beat you, but it's not so hard to stall you until Hoshiguma gets back. This is my house, after all.
Also, it'll be a good chance to try out the reforged Hannya.
Tameshigiri? Wonderful.
I have something to show you before that, though.
Mifune reaches into his clothes.
Tessai keeps a careful eye on his movements, but all Mifune does is take out a few photos, casually tossing them to the floor.
Tessai catches a glimpse of one of the photos, which falls face-up in front of him—
Tetsuya?!
Tetsuya.
Such a good boy. He did everything I asked of him to the limits of his ability, whether he could ultimately handle it or not.
For example, he really did find the Northern Court spy... himself.
Why—you?!
I was surprised when I first saw the photos, but then it started to make sense.
Tetsuya's mother may have missed her family in the north, but she steadfastly refused any approach to become a spy.
Yet the mark of Hoashi was on all the weapons that we, the Kinsekikai, procured in secret.
My fault, I suppose. The Kinsekikai's TV station wasn't as powerful as it is now, and those who wanted a piece of the Kinsekikai would have set upon the news like hungry beasts.
Eventually, I helped you find a place for the weapons, but you had to take care of the aftermath. You were forced to place the blame on Tetsuya's mother, as you once placed it on Hoshiguma.
To you, the Kinsekikai is the King on the shogi board.
You will preserve it at all costs, never hesitating to cut other pieces loose, be it a Queen like Hoshiguma, or Promoted Rooks like me that you look down on.
I'm curious myself... what does Tetsuya mean to you?
Is he a Rook? A Bishop? Or is he so incompetent that you see him as a Pawn you wouldn't mind losing?
If that was really so, we would have to have a fight here and now. But he isn't, is he?
He is incompetent, but he's not a shogi piece. He's your precious son, not to be placed on the board—
Shut up!
Clearly on edge, the old man seems ready to charge at Mifune with shield raised at any moment.
But Mifune can see Hannya trembling in his hands.
His mother's name will never be cleared. If the same fate befalls your only remaining child...
I said, SHUT UP!
You're hesitating. You're contemplating whether there's a chance the reforged Hannya will allow you to neutralize me in one blow, and bargain my life for Tetsuya's.
Chance. What an obnoxious word.
Are you scared?
Unfortunately for you, there is more than one person who carries out my will.
I'm not claiming strength in numbers, but the moment you swing Hannya at me, a button will be pressed, and these photos will show up on Kinseki Channel.
And you'll be free, once and for all, when Tetsuya realizes that he was right, and his father never cared about him, just as his father never cared about his mother.
What do you say, Old Man?
What do you say, Old Man?
What...?
No, it's not true! It can't be!
Tell me this isn't real!
Tetsuya frantically shakes the shoulders of the ninja who brought him here, but he cannot even tell if the ninja is looking at him.
After so many years, you have finally delivered Hannya into the hands of its true master.
Unfortunately, I still have to complete certain task entrusted to me. Where is the deed to Kajimachi? No rush, the one who gave me the task wanted it done cleanly. For now, you can simply follow me.
Those two...
The girls? I'm certainly willing to do you a favor, since you've been cooperative so far. Come quietly, and I'll spare their lives.
You can go to hell, Kinbee! I'll cut your tail off and make a belt out of it—
Kichisei!
His father's angry voice comes through the TV's speakers somewhat distorted, giving the scene a sense of surrealness.
Papa... Papa!
Where are you taking him? Let him go! You want to get me, come for me! Why him? Why?!
Mio-san. Where is the Ao-Oni?
Mifune-san's orders were to let her go when the time is right, and allow her to return to Kajimachi.
What of the boy? When do we let him go? Or are there other plans?
I don't know. The orders from upstairs were to keep him in the room, and don't let him break anything in it. I wasn't told anything else.
I see.
Mio-san... see, I'm not sure how long I'll have to watch him, and I was up all night last night... I'm very tired, to tell the truth...
You can go.
Arigatou!
What's on the TV?
Let me go.
I asked a question.
Let me go!
Mifune-san's orders are that you are not to leave this room.
My father's gone with your people! He's yielded! What else do you want?!
I'm simply following orders.
The young Oni draws his bokutou, but the ethereal hand emerging from behind the Liberi easily brings him to the ground.
Don't waste your energy.
Won't you say something, Chairman Tessai?
Have you decided to stay silent for now? Suit yourself, as long as you speak when I tell you to.
The camera follows Tessai.
Tetsuya sees his father leave the manor and enter a car stopped in front of its gate.
After a short while, the door of the car opens. Through an unremarkable door is a splendorous meeting hall.
What's this place?
Auction hall.
Auctioning what? Kajimachi? You don't mean my father's...?
No.
The next item for auction is not listed in the catalog. It was added at the last minute.
I present... the very first ever TV set manufactured by the Kinseki Group!
Starting at... s-seven million!
A TV set that looks so unremarkable, yet starting at the remarkable price of two thousand ryou... there must be something extraordinary about it!
Seven million, five hundred thousand.
Kouhei Mifune san? Are you serious?
Of course.
I doubt I'm the only one who recognizes its value.
A-Are you sure?
Regardless... we have a bid for seven million, five hundred thousand from Kouhei Mifune-san!
Seven million five hundred thousand going once... seven million five hundred thousand going twice...
...Ten million.
In the middle of the screen, the aging Oni slowly raises the paddle in his hand.
His old, feeble voice comes through the TV speakers.
That's... Chairman Tessai of the Kinsekikai?!
What did Mifune say... fifteen million? He's still raising the bid? What is he trying to do, kill my father by putting him into debt?
The Liberi says nothing, but the young man sees a hint of pity in her eyes that goes a little further than what the number 'fifteen million' should elicit.
Two hundred million?! You are bidding two hundred million, Tessai-san?!
Two...
Two hundred million?!
Two hundred million going once... two hundred million going twice... Last chance at two hundred million... sold.
Tetsuya watches as Tessai rises from his seat and walks past rows of well-dressed buyers, towards the TV set that should have belonged to him to begin with.
Tessai-san, there is still paperwork to be done...
Tessai ignores the auctioneer and gently lifts the TV set, the way he did when he marketed it to potential buyers, one-at-a-time, many years ago.
Tetsuya raises his bokutou again.
He charges at Mio in desperation, but is once again thrown easily to the floor.
Again, and again...
The TV set continues to mercilessly send distorted sounds into the room.
The sound of camera shutters, of people giving a show of applauding, of footsteps, of the rustling of pen on paper, of car doors shutting, of car engines rumbling.
The now-unfamiliar sound of the door to his father's manor.
Tetsuya has lost count of how many times he has gotten back up after being thrown to the ground by Mio. He charges futilely again, when he hears the voices behind him.
One, like a ghost; the other, like a man-eating Akki.
Voices that hardly even seem human anymore.
Now, give me the deed.
Sign here, stamp your handprint, and all will be done.
You still can't do it, can you?
But you understand that none of it is over, until you've put your palm to the page.
And what happens when I do?
The deed to Kajimachi will become the collateral on a loan in the amount of two hundred million. The TV set is yours, once you give the payment to the auction house—
It's Tetsuya that I'm talking about!
Him? Same as always. Those photos of him will be sealed away forever.
You'll never be able to pay back the debt. We can keep this secret together, and he'll be none the wiser.
The two people in the room with the TV both freeze.
Even the Liberi, who should have seen plenty of such things, takes an involuntary step backward.
You liar!
He's a liar! Mifune is a liar! Don't do it! No!
I know. It's not easy, choosing between abandoning your son and a crushing debt.
Money? I don't care about money...
Money. Business. Titles. Land. None of that matters anymore.
But only this... bowing my head to rotten scum like you...
And yet bow you will.
Bow you will, for the Tetsuya you failed to save from the rampaging Akki, and for the other Tetsuya you almost made a traitor out of.
Promise me. You won't kill Tetsuya, or Hoshiguma, or those two girls.
I won't.
He tricked me! He tricked me! Don't let him trick you!
You're the oyabun of the Kinsekikai! You're Tessai the Kingpin! You're my father! Papa! Papa!
This isn't supposed to happen! This isn't who you are!
You're the villain who can turn Mitsukue's yakuza upside-down with a wave of your hand! You're the steel-hearted murderer who killed my mother! This isn't who you are!
Papa—
As the old man places his hand on the inkpad, then leaves his red handprint on the paper, it seems like all life has been sucked out of him.
Good. Very good.
By the way, your son just saw everything that happened, from beginning to end. I'm sure he'll be grateful to you. No need to thank me.
You—how dare you?!
Your son thought he was the same as me. He thought you always looked down on him as you looked down on me, thinking of him as a worthless substitute.
I hope he learned his lesson, and stops thinking of himself as the most pitiable man under Heaven.
How lucky he is, to have someone willing to trample the last vestige of his own dignity for him.
A wave of the fan, and the hand-printed sheet is cut in half, but Mifune continues to sneer.
The image on the TV starts shaking. Something seems to have moved the camera.
Here's the camera.
You... you deceived me? You let Tetsuya know about all this... you never meant to spare him?
Why? Why would you deceive Hoshiguma, deceive me, deceive Tetsuya?!
'Deceive' is such a strong word.
Hoshiguma learned that her choice was only ever wishful thinking.
Tetsuya learned that you did love him, after all.
And you—you understand now, don't you?
Mitsukue no longer needs you or the hypocrisy of the Kinsekikai. Everything you've done was to fool yourself—
A loud noise comes through the speakers as the camera is smashed. The screen goes dark, but the speakers continue to mercilessly communicate the sound.
The sound of Tessai's fan clashing with Mifune's sword.
The sound of labored footsteps.
The sound of blade impaling flesh.
...Papa?
The sound of blood dripping.
Papa—
The sound of a body hitting the floor.
Papa!!!
Die on the battlefield... or... or die fighting cops, die to my vengeful blade! Don't die like this—
Don't—
Don't die for me like this—humiliated... trampled beneath another man's feet...
Y-You don't deserve to be my father. But I don't deserve to have you die for me like this!
Why would you do this for me? Why would you do this for a fool?!
Papa...
Mifune-san...?
Yes, I took over from the ninja.
His reaction...? I'm sure you can imagine.
You... you have no further use for him?
—Yes! What other orders do you have?
Yes, I will go to the place where Momoka is being held.
But was there not someone else? The woman by the name of Kichisei—
Mio, is it?
Mifune told you to kill me, didn't he?
He wants to wrap things up cleanly. He doesn't want any loose ends, does he?
Then do it. Kill me here and now.
Otherwise, I'll kill you with my tooth and nail, whatever it takes to get out of here.
Go.
Go. Before I change my mind.
Never come back.
Has the sun set?
Tessai does not know.
He has lost track of time.
He remembers falling to Mifune's blade. He remembers Mifune grabbing his hand and leaving a bloody handprint on a new contract.
Then, the footsteps grow distant, and his vision grows grey and blurry.
He wants to be free of all this—
Uncle Tessai?!

The old man stands back up feebly as he hears the familiar voice, perhaps out of guilt, perhaps out of lingering sentiment, perhaps because he does not want Hoshiguma to see the true extent of his weakness.
...Hoshiguma?
Who did this? Kinbee?!
Hoshiguma... I couldn't protect Kajimachi... or Hannya...
Where's Momoka? Kichisei?
Kinbee was right about one thing... sitting in my chair... I started thinking of everything as shogi pieces...
Moving them... trading them... sacrificing them...
But I forgot... I'm no more than a shogi piece myself, in the eyes of others...
Run... you're no match for Kinbee... leave this forsaken place... go back... go back to Lungmen...
No, I won't!
Sit down, Uncle Tessai. I'll find a doctor—

*cough* *cough* Don't... bother...
Can't you see... I'm beyond help...?
Uncle Tessai—
I'm here, Uncle Tessai. I'm not going anywhere.
Sit down. We'll sit together for a little bit... just like old times.
Hoshiguma helps the dying old man sit down.
